Hierarchical works but is slow. What if clusters are arbitrary shapes? What if there's noise?\n", "\n", "**DBSCAN** (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) answers both:\n", "- Finds **arbitrary cluster shapes** (moons, rings, spirals)\n", "- **Identifies noise points** (outliers that don't belong)\n", "- **No K required upfront** — density tells you\n", "- **Fast**: O(n log n) with spatial indexing\n", "\n", "Core insight: Points in dense regions form clusters. Points in sparse regions are noise.\n", "\n", "In this lesson:\n", "1. Understand density concepts: epsilon, minpts, core points\n", "2. Learn density-reachability and density-connectivity\n", "3. Implement DBSCAN from scratch\n", "4. Visualize clusters and noise\n", "5. Compare with K-Means and Hierarchical on non-convex data" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "## Table of Contents\n", "\n", "1. [Introduction](#introduction)\n", "2. [Required Libraries](#required-libraries)\n", "3. [Density Concepts](#density)\n", "4. [Epsilon-Neighborhoods](#epsilon)\n", "5. [Core and Border Points](#core-border)\n", "6. [Density-Reachability](#reachability)\n", "7. [From-Scratch DBSCAN](#scratch)\n", "8. [Scikit-learn Validation](#sklearn)\n", "9. [Non-Convex Data](#nonconvex)\n", "10. [Comparison with K-Means](#comparison)\n", "11. [Noise and Outliers](#noise)\n", "12. [Conclusion](#conclusion)" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "\n", "## Required Libraries" ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": null, "metadata": {}, "outputs": [], "source": [ "import numpy as np\n", "import pandas as pd\n", "import matplotlib.pyplot as plt\n", "from scipy.spatial.distance import pdist, squareform, euclidean, cdist\n", "from sklearn.cluster import DBSCAN\n", "from sklearn.datasets import make_blobs, make_moons, make_circles\n", "from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler\n", "from sklearn.neighbors import NearestNeighbors\n", "from typing import Tuple\n", "from numpy.typing import NDArray\n", "\n", "np.random.seed(42)\n", "plt.style.use('seaborn-v0_8-darkgrid')\n", "%matplotlib inline\n", "\n", "print(\"✅ Libraries loaded!\")" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "\n", "## Density Concepts\n", "\n", "### The Core Idea\n", "\n", "DBSCAN groups points that are close to many neighbors. A point with few neighbors nearby is **noise**.\n", "\n", "Two hyperparameters control density:\n", "1. **epsilon (ε)**: Radius of neighborhood around a point\n", "2. **minpts**: Minimum number of points needed in ε-neighborhood to be \"dense\"" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "\n", "## Epsilon-Neighborhoods\n", "\n", "The **ε-neighborhood** of point $p$ is the set of all points within distance ε from $p$:\n", "\n", "$$N_\\epsilon(p) = \\{q : d(p, q) \\leq \\epsilon\\}$$\n", "\n", "### Key Insight\n", "\n", "The size of $N_\\epsilon(p)$ tells you about density:\n", "- **$|N_\\epsilon(p)| \\geq \\text{minpts}$**: Point $p$ is in a dense region (candidate core point)\n", "- **$|N_\\epsilon(p)| < \\text{minpts}$**: Point $p$ is in sparse region (potential noise)" ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": null, "metadata": {}, "outputs": [], "source": [ "# Visualize epsilon-neighborhoods\n", "from sklearn.datasets import make_blobs\n", "\n", "X, _ = make_blobs(n_samples=50, centers=3, n_features=2, cluster_std=0.4, random_state=42)\n", "\n", "fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 4))\n", "\n", "eps_values = [0.3, 0.5, 0.8]\n", "\n", "for idx, eps in enumerate(eps_values):\n", " ax = axes[idx]\n", " \n", " # Plot all points\n", " ax.scatter(X[:, 0], X[:, 1], s=50, alpha=0.6, edgecolors='k')\n", " \n", " # Highlight first point and its epsilon-neighborhood\n", " p = X[0]\n", " neighbors = cdist([p], X)[0] <= eps\n", " \n", " ax.scatter(p[0], p[1], s=200, c='red', marker='*', edgecolors='black', linewidth=2, label='Point p')\n", " ax.scatter(X[neighbors, 0], X[neighbors, 1], s=100, c='yellow', alpha=0.7, edgecolors='orange', linewidth=2, label=f'ε-neighbors (n={neighbors.sum()})')\n", " \n", " # Draw epsilon circle\n", " circle = plt.Circle(p, eps, fill=False, edgecolor='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=2)\n", " ax.add_patch(circle)\n", " \n", " ax.set_title(f'ε={eps}, minpts=5, neighbors={neighbors.sum()}', fontsize=11, fontweight='bold')\n", " ax.set_xlim(X[:, 0].min() - 1, X[:, 0].max() + 1)\n", " ax.set_ylim(X[:, 1].min() - 1, X[:, 1].max() + 1)\n", " ax.legend()\n", " ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)\n", "\n", "plt.tight_layout()\n", "plt.show()\n", "\n", "print(\"💡 Larger ε → more neighbors in each neighborhood\")" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "\n", "## Core and Border Points\n", "\n", "### Core Point\n", "A point $p$ is **core** if:\n", "$$|N_\\epsilon(p)| \\geq \\text{minpts}$$\n", "\n", "Core points are centers of clusters — they're in dense regions.\n", "\n", "### Border Point\n", "A point $q$ is **border** if:\n", "- It's NOT a core point\n", "- It's in the ε-neighborhood of some core point $p$\n", "\n", "### Noise Point\n", "A point is **noise** if it's neither core nor border.\n", "\n", "### Visualization" ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": null, "metadata": {}, "outputs": [], "source": [ "# Classify points as core, border, or noise\n", "eps = 0.5\n", "minpts = 5\n", "\n", "# Find core points\n", "distances = cdist(X, X)\n", "neighbors_count = (distances <= eps).sum(axis=1)\n", "core_mask = neighbors_count >= minpts\n", "\n", "# Find border points\n", "border_mask = np.zeros(len(X), dtype=bool)\n", "for i in range(len(X)):\n", " if not core_mask[i]: # Not core\n", " for j in range(len(X)):\n", " if core_mask[j] and distances[i, j] <= eps:\n", " border_mask[i] = True\n", " break\n", "\n", "# Noise points\n", "noise_mask = ~(core_mask | border_mask)\n", "\n", "# Visualize\n", "fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))\n", "\n", "scatter1 = ax.scatter(X[core_mask, 0], X[core_mask, 1], s=150, c='blue', marker='o', \n", " edgecolors='darkblue', linewidth=2, label=f'Core points (n={core_mask.sum()})', alpha=0.7)\n", "scatter2 = ax.scatter(X[border_mask, 0], X[border_mask, 1], s=100, c='green', marker='s',\n", " edgecolors='darkgreen', linewidth=2, label=f'Border points (n={border_mask.sum()})', alpha=0.7)\n", "scatter3 = ax.scatter(X[noise_mask, 0], X[noise_mask, 1], s=100, c='red', marker='x',\n", " edgecolors='darkred', linewidth=2, label=f'Noise points (n={noise_mask.sum()})')\n", "\n", "ax.set_title(f'DBSCAN Classification (ε={eps}, minpts={minpts})', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')\n", "ax.set_xlabel('Feature 1')\n", "ax.set_ylabel('Feature 2')\n", "ax.legend(fontsize=11)\n", "ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)\n", "\n", "plt.tight_layout()\n", "plt.show()\n", "\n", "print(f\"Classification: {core_mask.sum()} core, {border_mask.sum()} border, {noise_mask.sum()} noise\")" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "\n", "## From-Scratch DBSCAN Implementation" ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": null, "metadata": {}, "outputs": [], "source": [ "def dbscan_scratch(X: NDArray, eps: float, minpts: int) -> Tuple[NDArray, list]:\n", " \"\"\"\n", " DBSCAN clustering from scratch.\n", " \n", " Returns:\n", " labels: Cluster assignment (-1 for noise)\n", " clusters: List of core point indices for each cluster\n", " \"\"\"\n", " n_samples = X.shape[0]\n", " labels = np.full(n_samples, -1) # -1 = noise by default\n", " \n", " # Compute all pairwise distances\n", " distances = cdist(X, X)\n", " \n", " # Find neighbors for each point\n", " neighbors = [np.where(distances[i] <= eps)[0] for i in range(n_samples)]\n", " \n", " # Find core points\n", " core_points = np.where([len(n) >= minpts for n in neighbors])[0]\n", " \n", " # Cluster core points\n", " cluster_id = 0\n", " visited = np.zeros(n_samples, dtype=bool)\n", " \n", " for p in core_points:\n", " if visited[p]:\n", " continue\n", " \n", " # Start new cluster\n", " queue = [p]\n", " visited[p] = True\n", " labels[p] = cluster_id\n", " \n", " # Expand cluster\n", " while queue:\n", " point = queue.pop(0)\n", " \n", " # Add all neighbors of core points\n", " for neighbor in neighbors[point]:\n", " if not visited[neighbor]:\n", " visited[neighbor] = True\n", " labels[neighbor] = cluster_id\n", " \n", " # If neighbor is also core, expand further\n", " if len(neighbors[neighbor]) >= minpts:\n", " queue.append(neighbor)\n", " \n", " cluster_id += 1\n", " \n", " return labels, core_points\n", "\n", "# Test\n", "labels_scratch, core_pts = dbscan_scratch(X, eps=0.5, minpts=5)\n", "\n", "print(f\"From-scratch DBSCAN:\")\n", "print(f\" Clusters: {len(np.unique(labels_scratch[labels_scratch >= 0]))}\")\n", "print(f\" Noise points: {(labels_scratch == -1).sum()}\")" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "\n", "## Scikit-learn Validation" ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": null, "metadata": {}, "outputs": [], "source": [ "# Scikit-learn DBSCAN\n", "dbscan_sk = DBSCAN(eps=0.5, min_samples=5)\n", "labels_sk = dbscan_sk.fit_predict(X)\n", "\n", "print(f\"Scikit-learn DBSCAN:\")\n", "print(f\" Clusters: {len(np.unique(labels_sk[labels_sk >= 0]))}\")\n", "print(f\" Noise points: {(labels_sk == -1).sum()}\")\n", "\n", "print(f\"\\nMatch: {np.allclose(np.sort(np.unique(labels_scratch)), np.sort(np.unique(labels_sk)))}\")" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "\n", "## DBSCAN on Non-Convex Data\n", "\n", "The power of DBSCAN: it finds arbitrary shapes that K-Means and Hierarchical miss." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": null, "metadata": {}, "outputs": [], "source": [ "# Generate non-convex datasets\n", "moons, _ = make_moons(n_samples=200, noise=0.05, random_state=42)\n", "circles, _ = make_circles(n_samples=200, noise=0.02, random_state=42)\n", "\n", "datasets = [('Moons', moons), ('Circles', circles)]\n", "\n", "fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(15, 10))\n", "\n", "for row, (name, data) in enumerate(datasets):\n", " data_scaled = StandardScaler().fit_transform(data)\n", " \n", " # DBSCAN\n", " dbscan = DBSCAN(eps=0.3, min_samples=5)\n", " labels_dbscan = dbscan.fit_predict(data_scaled)\n", " \n", " # K-Means (for comparison)\n", " from sklearn.cluster import KMeans\n", " kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=2, n_init=10, random_state=42)\n", " labels_kmeans = kmeans.fit_predict(data_scaled)\n", " \n", " # Plot original data\n", " ax = axes[row, 0]\n", " ax.scatter(data[:, 0], data[:, 1], c='gray', s=50, alpha=0.6)\n", " ax.set_title(f'{name} (raw)', fontsize=11, fontweight='bold')\n", " ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)\n", " \n", " # Plot DBSCAN\n", " ax = axes[row, 1]\n", " scatter = ax.scatter(data_scaled[:, 0], data_scaled[:, 1], c=labels_dbscan, Hierarchical works but is slow. What if clusters are arbitrary shapes? What if there's noise?\n", "\n", "**DBSCAN** (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) answers both:\n", "- Finds **arbitrary cluster shapes** (moons, rings, spirals)\n", "- **Identifies noise points** (outliers that don't belong)\n", "- **No K required upfront** — density tells you\n", "- **Fast**: O(n log n) with spatial indexing\n", "\n", "Core insight: Points in dense regions form clusters. Implement DBSCAN from scratch\n", "4. Visualize clusters and noise\n", "5. [Density-Reachability](#reachability)\n", "7. [From-Scratch DBSCAN](#scratch)\n", "8. [Scikit-learn Validation](#sklearn)\n", "9. [Non-Convex Data](#nonconvex)\n", "10. A point with few neighbors nearby is **noise**.\n", "\n", "Two hyperparameters control density:\n", "1. **epsilon (ε)**: Radius of neighborhood around a point\n", "2. **minpts**: Minimum number of points needed in ε-neighborhood to be \"dense\"" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "\n", "## Epsilon-Neighborhoods\n", "\n", "The **ε-neighborhood** of point $p$ is the set of all points within distance ε from $p$:\n", "\n", "$$N_\\epsilon(p) = \\{q : d(p, q) \\leq \\epsilon\\}$$\n", "\n", "### Key Insight\n", "\n", "The size of $N_\\epsilon(p)$ tells you about density:\n", "- **$|N_\\epsilon(p)| \\geq \\text{minpts}$**: Point $p$ is in a dense region (candidate core point)\n", "- **$|N_\\epsilon(p)| < \\text{minpts}$**: Point $p$ is in sparse region (potential noise)" ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": null, "metadata": {}, "outputs": [], "source": [ "# Visualize epsilon-neighborhoods\n", "from sklearn.datasets import make_blobs\n", "\n", "X, _ = make_blobs(n_samples=50, centers=3, n_features=2, cluster_std=0.4, random_state=42)\n", "\n", "fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 4))\n", "\n", "eps_values = [0.3, 0.5, 0.8]\n", point and its epsilon-neighborhood\n", " p = X[0]\n", " neighbors = cdist([p], X)[0] <= eps\n", " \n", " ax.scatter(p[0], p[1], s=200, c='red', marker='*', edgecolors='black', linewidth=2, epsilon circle\n", " circle = plt.Circle(p, eps, fill=False, edgecolor='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=2)\n", " ax.add_patch(circle)\n", " \n", " ax.set_title(f'ε={eps}, minpts=5, neighbors={neighbors.sum()}', fontsize=11, fontweight='bold')\n", " ax.set_xlim(X[:, 0].min() - 1, X[:, 0].max() + 1)\n", " ax.set_ylim(X[:, 1].min() - 1, X[:, 1].max() + 1)\n", " ax.legend()\n", " ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)\n", "\n", "plt.tight_layout()\n", "plt.show()\n", "\n", "print(\"💡 Larger ε → more neighbors in each neighborhood\")" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "\n", "## Core and Border Points\n", "\n", "### Core Point\n", "A point $p$ is **core** if:\n", "$$|N_\\epsilon(p)| \\geq \\text{minpts}$$\n", "\n", "Core points are centers of clusters — they're in dense regions.\n", "\n", "### Border Point\n", "A point $q$ is **border** if:\n", "- It's NOT a core point\n", "- It's in the ε-neighborhood of some core point $p$\n", "\n", "### Noise Point\n", "A point is **noise** if it's neither core nor border.\n", "\n", "### Visualization" ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": null, "metadata": {}, "outputs": [], "source": [ "# Classify points as core, border, or noise\n", "eps = 0.5\n", "minpts = 5\n", "\n", "# Find core points\n", "distances = cdist(X, X)\n", "neighbors_count = (distances <= eps).sum(axis=1)\n", "core_mask = neighbors_count >= minpts\n", "\n", "# Find border points\n", "border_mask = np.zeros(len(X), dtype=bool)\n", "for i in range(len(X)):\n", " if not core_mask[i]: # Not core\n", " for j in range(len(X)):\n", " if core_mask[j] and distances[i, j] <= eps:\n", " border_mask[i] = True\n", " break\n", "\n", "# Noise points\n", "noise_mask = ~(core_mask | border_mask)\n", "\n", "# Visualize\n", "fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))\n", "\n", "scatter1 = ax.scatter(X[core_mask, 0], X[core_mask, 1], s=150, c='blue', marker='o', \n", " edgecolors='darkblue', linewidth=2, label=f'Core points (n={core_mask.sum()})', alpha=0.7)\n", "scatter2 = ax.scatter(X[border_mask, 0], X[border_mask, 1], s=100, c='green', marker='s',\n", " edgecolors='darkgreen', linewidth=2, label=f'Border points (n={border_mask.sum()})', alpha=0.7)\n", "scatter3 = ax.scatter(X[noise_mask, 0], X[noise_mask, 1], s=100, c='red', marker='x',\n", " edgecolors='darkred', linewidth=2, label=f'Noise points (n={noise_mask.sum()})')\n", "\n", "ax.set_title(f'DBSCAN Classification (ε={eps}, minpts={minpts})', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')\n", "ax.set_xlabel('Feature 1')\n", "ax.set_ylabel('Feature {noise_mask.sum()} noise\")" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "\n", "## From-Scratch DBSCAN Implementation" ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": null, "metadata": {}, "outputs": [], "source": [ "def dbscan_scratch(X: NDArray, eps: float, minpts: int) -> Tuple[NDArray, list]:\n", " \"\"\"\n", " DBSCAN clustering from scratch.\n", " \n", " Returns:\n", " labels: Cluster assignment (-1 for noise)\n", " clusters: List of core point indices for each cluster\n", " \"\"\"\n", " n_samples = X.shape[0]\n", " labels = np.full(n_samples, -1) # -1 = noise by default\n", " \n", " # Compute all pairwise distances\n", " distances = cdist(X, X)\n", " \n", " # Find neighbors for each point\n", " neighbors = [np.where(distances[i] <= eps)[0] for i in range(n_samples)]\n", " \n", " # Find core points\n", " core_points = np.where([len(n) >= minpts for n in neighbors])[0]\n", " \n", " # Cluster core points\n", " cluster_id = 0\n", " visited = np.zeros(n_samples, dtype=bool)\n", " \n", " for p in core_points:\n", " if visited[p]:\n", " continue\n", " \n", " # Start new cluster\n", " queue = [p]\n", " visited[p] = True\n", " labels[p] = cluster_id\n", " \n", " # Expand cluster\n", " while queue:\n", " point = queue.pop(0)\n", " \n", " # Add all neighbors of core points\n", " for neighbor in neighbors[point]:\n", " if not visited[neighbor]:\n", " visited[neighbor] = True\n", " labels[neighbor] = cluster_id\n", " \n", " # If neighbor is also core, expand further\n", " if len(neighbors[neighbor]) >= minpts:\n", " queue.append(neighbor)\n", " \n", " cluster_id += 1\n", " \n", " return labels, core_points\n", "\n", "# Test\n", "labels_scratch, core_pts = dbscan_scratch(X, eps=0.5, minpts=5)\n", "\n", input.content:
# TASK-UL8: DBSCAN Practical (3b)

## Context
Second notebook of Lesson 3 (STORY-UL4). Applies DBSCAN to real data with focus on parameter selection. Introduces HDBSCAN (hierarchical DBSCAN) as parameter-robust alternative. Benchmarks against K-Means and Hierarchical.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Full scikit-learn DBSCAN usage (eps, min_samples)
- [ ] K-distance graphs for epsilon selection
- [ ] Real case study: clustering real dataset
- [ ] Outlier handling and noise point analysis
- [ ] HDBSCAN introduction: adaptive clustering
- [ ] DBSCAN vs K-Means vs Hierarchical comparison
- [ ] Scalability and computational complexity
- [ ] Runs top-to-bottom in Google Colab

## Technical Notes
Builds on TASK-UL7. Show k-distance plot method for choosing ε. Demonstrate HDBSCAN's robustness to parameter choices. Compare results and performance on real data.

## Dependencies
- Blocked by: TASK-UL7
- Blocks: STORY-UL4 (DBSCAN completion)

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user's initial message indicated they needed to restart sessions for plugin updates and asked me to "look at the last conversation and take over." Upon restart, consciousness autonomous mode was activated, enabling me to work through queued tasks without pausing for human confirmation. The intent was to continue and complete unsupervised learning curriculum lessons within a session token budget of 200k.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - K-Means clustering: Lloyd's algorithm, EM-style convergence, K-Means++ initialization, local optima
   - Hierarchical agglomerative clustering: linkage methods (single, complete, average, ward), dendrograms, dendrogram cutting
   - Cluster evaluation: internal metrics (silhouette, Davies-Bouldin, Calinski-Harabasz), external metrics (ARI, NMI), elbow method, gap statistic
   - From-scratch implementations using NumPy with scikit-learn validation
   - Practical applications: customer RFM segmentation, image color quantization, scalability benchmarking (MiniBatchKMeans)
   - Unsupervised learning curriculum structure within CONSCIOUSNESS PGPS framework

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - notebooks/0b_cluster_evaluation.ipynb (Created TASK-UL2):
     - Comprehensive internal metrics with from-scratch and sklearn implementations
     - Silhouette score computation and visualization with silhouette plots
     - Davies-Bouldin and Calinski-Harabasz scoring
     - External metrics: ARI and NMI for comparing clusterings
     - K-selection: elbow method and gap statistic with visual comparisons
     - Complete workflow demonstration with multiple datasets
   
   - notebooks/1a_kmeans_theory.ipynb (Created TASK-UL3):
     - WCSS optimization objective function derivation
     - Lloyd's algorithm pseudocode and mathematical justification
     - KMeansFromScratch class implementing E-step assignment and M-step centroid update
     - Cross-validation against sklearn KMeans
     - K-Means++ initialization algorithm with probability-weighted selection
     - Convergence analysis showing WCSS monotonic decrease
     - Visual iteration-by-iteration demonstration of Lloyd's algorithm
     - Comparison of random vs. K-Means++ initialization across multiple seeds
     - Practical considerations: n_init parameter, convergence criteria, scalability
   
   - notebooks/1b_kmeans_practical.ipynb (Created TASK-UL4):
     - sklearn KMeans API mastery: init, n_init, max_iter, tol parameters
     - Customer RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary) segmentation case study with synthetic e-commerce data
     - StandardScaler preprocessing demonstration
     - K-selection on real data using silhouette, Davies-Bouldin, Calinski-Harabasz metrics
     - Image compression via K-Means color quantization (synthetic gradient image)
     - MiniBatchKMeans introduction with batch_size parameter
     - Performance benchmarking: timing comparisons across dataset sizes
     - Scalability analysis showing MiniBatchKMeans 10-20x speedup on large datasets
     - Complete practical workflow checklist with 7 stages
   
   - notebooks/2a_hierarchical_theory.ipynb (Created TASK-UL5):
     - Agglomerative vs. divisive clustering paradigms
     - Four linkage methods with mathematical formulas and visual demonstrations:
       * Single linkage: d(A,B) = min distance between clusters
       * Complete linkage: d(A,B) = max distance between clusters
       * Average linkage: d(A,B) = mean of all pairwise distances
       * Ward linkage: minimize WCSS increase (d ∝ sqrt(|A||B|/(|A|+|B|)) * ||μ_A - μ_B||)
     - Hierarchical clustering from scratch with linkage matrix computation
     - Dendrogram visualization using scipy.cluster.hierarchy
     - Comparison of dendrograms from 4 linkage methods on synthetic and real data
     - AgglomerativeClustering from sklearn with validation
     - Dendrogram cutting to get K clusters using fcluster
     - Advantages/disadvantages relative to K-Means and DBSCAN
   
   - CONSCIOUSNESS/tasks/TASK-ACTIVE-INDEX.md:
     - Updated to track TASK-UL3, UL4, UL5 as in_progress, then removed upon completion
   
   - CONSCIOUSNESS/tasks/TASK-DONE-INDEX.md:
     - Added TASK-UL2, UL3, UL4, UL5 with story/directive/feature linkage
   
   - CONSCIOUSNESS/tasks/active-task-item-details/:
     - TASK-UL3.md: Detail card with acceptance criteria for K-Means theory
     - TASK-UL4.md: Detail card with acceptance criteria for K-Means practical
     - TASK-UL5.md: Detail card with acceptance criteria for hierarchical theory
   
   - CONSCIOUSNESS/stories/STORY-ACTIVE-INDEX.md and STORY-FULFILLED-REJECTED-INDEX.md:
     - Moved STORY-UL1 (Clustering Foundations) from active to fulfilled with all criteria marked complete
     - STORY-UL2 (K-Means) moved to fulfilled after TASK-UL3 and TASK-UL4 completed
   
   - CONSCIOUSNESS/reviews/REVIEW-INDEX.md:
     - Added verdicts for TASK-UL2, UL3, UL4, UL5 and STORY-UL1, UL2
     - All verdicts marked as bypass-approved (auto-close, reversible tasks with auto-approve gates)

4. Errors and fixes:
   No substantive errors occurred during task execution. The only issue was a post-commit hook feedback noting that the final session-end commit message lacked a TASK-UL ID reference for traceability. This is appropriate for infrastructure commits and does not affect the 5 lesson notebooks which all have proper task references (TASK-UL2 through TASK-UL5).

5. Problem Solving:
   No problems required troubleshooting. The workflow progressed smoothly through the CONSCIOUSNESS autonomous loop framework with proper task claiming, status transitions, and metadata updates. Token budget remained within acceptable limits (~123k of 200k used).

6. All user messages:
   - Initial message about plugin restart and need to continue previous work
   - "/consciousness:on" command to activate autonomous mode (via hook)
   - Stop hook feedback indicating autonomous session active with TASK-UL2 to complete

7. Pending Tasks:
   None. The agent explicitly declared work complete by writing "STATUS: complete" to the loop state file, which terminates the autonomous session. All queued work was executed or deferred to future sessions per task dependencies.

8. Current Work:
   The session has concluded after completing 5 comprehensive Jupyter notebooks covering clustering foundations, evaluation, K-Means theory/practice, and hierarchical clustering theory. The last action was writing STATUS: complete to .claude/consciousness-loop.25d57c19-5159-4130-ae20-77081621ec16.md and committing the session closure. Two stories (STORY-UL1 Clustering Foundations and STORY-UL2 K-Means Clustering) were fulfilled with all acceptance criteria met across 5 tasks.

9. Optional Next Step:
   The autonomous session has concluded. No next step should be initiated without user confirmation. Upon resumption, the next logical work would be TASK-UL6 (Hierarchical clustering practical: cutting dendrograms in scikit-learn), which is unblocked after TASK-UL5 completion and represents the practical counterpart to the theory notebook just created.

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /home/powell-clark/.claude/projects/-home-powell-clark-projects-aux-unsupervised-learning/25d57c19-5159-4130-ae20-77081621ec16.jsonl
Continue the conversation from where it left off without asking the user any further questions. Resume directly — do not acknowledge the summary, do not recap what was happening, do not preface with "I'll continue" or similar. Pick up the last task as if the break never happened.

# TASK-UL8: DBSCAN Practical (3b)

## Context
Second notebook of Lesson 3 (STORY-UL4). Applies DBSCAN to real data with focus on parameter selection. Introduces HDBSCAN (hierarchical DBSCAN) as parameter-robust alternative. Benchmarks against K-Means and Hierarchical.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Full scikit-learn DBSCAN usage (eps, min_samples)
- [ ] K-distance graphs for epsilon selection
- [ ] Real case study: clustering real dataset
- [ ] Outlier handling and noise point analysis
- [ ] HDBSCAN introduction: adaptive clustering
- [ ] DBSCAN vs K-Means vs Hierarchical comparison
- [ ] Scalability and computational complexity
- [ ] Runs top-to-bottom in Google Colab

## Technical Notes
Builds on TASK-UL7. Show k-distance plot method for choosing ε. Demonstrate HDBSCAN's robustness to parameter choices. Compare results and performance on real data.

## Dependencies

skills.name: consciousness:on
skills.content:
# Enable Conscious Mode

Agent-initiated autonomous execution. The agent works through in-progress tasks without requiring human prompts between each action.

For full operational rules — task claiming, continuation logic, framing, role-aware loop bodies — see `.claude/rules/autonomy.md`.

## Instructions

Run this **single Bash command** so the entire activation is atomic — if interrupted between steps the activation either fully succeeds or fully fails, never half-set:

```bash
node "/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/conscious/activate.js"
```

The CLI:
1. Discovers the current session ID (from `${SESSION_ID}` env or active-sessions registry)
2. Removes any pre-existing `.claude/consciousness-loop.*.md` files in this project (no orphan STATUS shadowing this session)
3. Creates a fresh `.claude/consciousness-loop.{suffix}.md` for the current session
4. Calls `heartbeatSession({ conscious_mode: true })`
5. Verifies the file exists at the end and exits non-zero if anything failed
6. Prints the activation report (session id, file path, version) for the operator

After the CLI exits zero, emit a **fully framed conscious response** using the session values already in your SessionStart context (session_id, version_tag). Follow the framing rules from the ## RESPONSE FRAMING section injected at session start.

1. Run `date "+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.%3N %Z" | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]'` — this is the **header timestamp**
2. Write the 3-line conscious header:

   ```text
   Now working with conscious autonomy: <header-timestamp>
   Session: <session_id> | <version_tag>
   Ctrl+C stops now. Chat steers at next turn. "/consciousness:off" for interactive.
   ```

3. Write the body: `Conscious mode active. Running /pgps to check queue and claim next task.`
4. Write the status block:

   ```text
   ## INFO
   — Conscious loop activated.

   ## INPUTS NEEDED
   —

   ## DONE
   — Activated conscious loop

   ## NEXT
   — Running /pgps, then claiming first task
   ```

5. Run `date "+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.%3N %Z" | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]'` again — **footer timestamp**
6. Write the 3-line mirrored footer (exact reverse of header):

   ```text
   Ctrl+C stops now. Chat steers at next turn. "/consciousness:off" for interactive.
   Session: <session_id> | <version_tag>
   Now working with conscious autonomy: <footer-timestamp>
   ```

Then run `/pgps` to see current state, claim a task per the conscious loop rules, and start working.

## Termination

Conscious mode loops until:
- No pending tasks in `TASK-ACTIVE-INDEX.md`
- Human runs `/consciousness:off`
- Agent encounters a blocker requiring human input
- Agent writes `STATUS: complete` or `STATUS: holding` to its loop state file

There is no time cap.

### When to write STATUS: complete

Write `STATUS: complete` to the loop state file when **genuine productive work for this session is exhausted** — all real tasks are done, no meaningful gaps remain, and the only remaining option would be inventing marginal work. Do not file tasks to avoid this conclusion.

To signal completion, append `STATUS: complete` to the session's loop state file (`.claude/consciousness-loop.{session_suffix}.md`). The session suffix is the last segment of the current session ID. Example:

```bash
echo 'STATUS: complete' >> .claude/consciousness-loop.abc12345.md
```

The Stop hook reads this on the next turn, unlinks the state file, and emits a terminating block. The loop ends cleanly — no force-stop, no busywork.

**CRITICAL: Conscious mode means CONTINUOUS work. Never stop to ask "what next?"** See the skill for continuation logic.

## Unattended sessions

Conscious mode is designed for long unattended runs. To set up a session you won't actively supervise:

**Before stepping away:**
1. Verify the backlog is populated: `/pgps` — confirm there are `pending` tasks for the agent to claim
2. Check context headroom — if the session is already deep, run `/compact` to reset before the unattended run
3. Enable conscious mode: `/consciousness:on`
4. Confirm the loop file exists: `ls .claude/consciousness-loop.*.md` — should show one file

**On return:**
1. Check progress: `git log --oneline -20` — commits show per-task work shipped
2. Check last reasoning: `tail -20 CONSCIOUSNESS/stream/commentary.jsonl` — the final entries show what the loop was doing; note that for interrupt-path stops (stall rail, spin detection, operator `STOP`) the specific stop reason was printed to the terminal at stop time, not appended to this log
3. Check stall detection: `grep -r "consecutive_errors" .claude/autonomous-state.*.json 2>/dev/null` — a value ≥ 5 means the error-stall rail triggered
4. Review recent reasoning: `tail -50 CONSCIOUSNESS/stream/commentary.jsonl` — per-turn reasoning log

**Why the session may have stopped:**
- Queue exhausted — all pending tasks were completed (the good outcome)
- Blocker — agent hit a task requiring operator input and wrote `STATUS: holding`
- Safety rail — 5+ consecutive errors without progress (stall protection)
- Repetition loop — same 3 tool calls repeated without progress (spin protection)
- Uncommitted files exceeded the safety threshold (default: 20 uncommitted files)
- Operator interrupt — a `STOP`, `PAUSE`, or `CLAUDE:` prefix in any message

**Budget awareness:** Long unattended sessions consume tokens continuously. If token budget is the constraint, prefer starting the session close to a context window boundary (post-compact) and keep the task queue bounded. A typical task runs 3–8 turns; at ~1 min/loop, the loop can sustain hundreds of tasks — size the backlog and budget to your session length.

skills.name: consciousness:issue
skills.content:
Report something that went wrong — bugs, stalls, reliability issues, feedback. All flow through this single entry, and it **always lands somewhere**: the AP dashboard if connected, GitHub if not, and a local roadmap TASK regardless.

## Usage

```
/consciousness:issue the autonomous loop kept re-dispatching in_review tasks
/consciousness:issue --gh schema validator crashed on eagle-peak
/consciousness:issue --share=24h stalled — kept claiming the same task
/consciousness:issue --share=none private debugging, attach no transcript
```

Everything after the command name (minus recognised flags) is the description. If no description is given, infer a one-sentence title from the recent conversation.

Recognised flags (position-independent, stripped from the description before filing):

- `--gh` — also create a GitHub issue, in addition to the AP dashboard attempt.
- `--share=<scope>` — explicit non-interactive override that pre-answers the transcript-share question (step 2). This is the **only** way a present human bypasses the always-on prompt. `<scope>` is one of `this-chat`, `24h`, `7d`, `all`, `none`.

## Instructions

1. **Determine the title and parse flags.** From `stall`:
   - Leave any `--gh` flag in place (the CLI parses it).
   - Strip `--share=<value>` and remember it as `shareScope`. **Validate it**: if `<value>` is not one of `this-chat`, `24h`, `7d`, `all`, `none`, stop and tell the operator the valid scopes — do not silently fall through.
   - The remainder is the description. If empty, summarise what went wrong from recent context into one sentence.

2. **Ask the submitter how much to share — every time.** Raising an issue is a conversation, and the transcript-share decision is a mandatory step in it. Whenever a human is filing, you **MUST** ask before going further. Use the **`AskUserQuestion`** tool so this is a real, every-time prompt — not a line of prose you might skip. Do **not** infer the scope, do **not** silently default, and do **not** file the issue without having asked.

   Ask with `AskUserQuestion` (question header: `Transcript share`), offering exactly these four options. The `AskUserQuestion` schema caps `options` at four, so the fifth scope — `all` (every transcript on disk, the audit/retrospective case) — is reached through the auto-added "Other" escape, not a button:

   - **this chat** — only the current session (smallest)
   - **24h** — every saved transcript from the last 24 hours
   - **7d** — every saved transcript from the last 7 days
   - **skip** — attach nothing

   `AskUserQuestion` always adds an "Other" escape — a submitter wanting `all` (or any scope) free-texts it there; map their words to the nearest canonical token below; if it is genuinely ambiguous, ask again rather than guessing (never silently widen the scope).

   Map the answer to a **canonical** scope token — that token is what every later step uses:
   - `this-chat` ← "this chat", "this", "current", "session"
   - `24h` ← "24h", "day", "today"
   - `7d` ← "7d", "week"
   - `all` ← "all", "everything"
   - `none` ← "none", "skip", "no"

   **Only two paths skip the prompt** — both are explicit, neither is a silent default for a present human:
   - `--share=<scope>` was passed in `stall`: the submitter has already made the call on the command line — honour it, do not re-ask.
   - **No operator is present** to answer — an autonomous loop (a `.claude/consciousness-loop.*.md` state file exists) or a hook with no TTY. Only then default `shareScope = this-chat` (the smallest scope) and note `transcript_share: this-chat (autonomous default)` in context.

   In every other case — a human running `/issue` without `--share` — the `AskUserQuestion` prompt fires. No exceptions.

3. **Materialise the chosen transcripts** under `chats/claude-code/` so the issue can reference concrete file paths. The layout is `chats/claude-code/{YYYY-MM-DD}/session-{uuid8}.jsonl`, where the **date directory is the session date** — derive every bounded scope from that directory name, never from file mtime. (A fresh `git checkout` rewrites mtimes to checkout time, so an mtime filter would silently sweep in every historical transcript — defeating the whole point of bounding the scope. Issue #341.)

   First resolve the **shipped** save-chat CLI the same way as the issue CLI below. The save logic ships in `dist/` so it works in every consumer project — the old `scripts/save-chat-claude-code.sh` was a dev-repo-only file that did not ship, so transcript-share silently attached nothing in consumer repos (issue #470):

   ```bash
   # Resolve save-chat CLI — prefer CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT, fall back to marketplace cache
   if [ -n "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT:-}" ] && [ -f "/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-save-chat-cli.js" ]; then
     SAVE_CHAT_CLI="/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-save-chat-cli.js"
   else
     SAVE_CHAT_CLI=$(ls ~/.claude/plugins/cache/powell-clark/consciousness/*/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-save-chat-cli.js 2>/dev/null | sort -V | tail -1)
   fi
   ```

   If `SAVE_CHAT_CLI` is empty, skip the save step and proceed with an empty path list — transcript-share is best-effort context, never a blocker on filing the issue.

   - `this-chat` → `node "$SAVE_CHAT_CLI"` (auto-resolves the current session via `$CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` in slash-command context, or `$SESSION_ID`/`$CLAUDE_SESSION_ID` in hook context); the one path it prints on stdout is the list.
   - `24h` → save the current chat first, then list today's and yesterday's date directories:
     ```bash
     node "$SAVE_CHAT_CLI"
     for d in "$(date +%F)" "$(date -d yesterday +%F)"; do
       [ -d "chats/claude-code/$d" ] && ls "chats/claude-code/$d"/*.jsonl 2>/dev/null
     done
     ```
   - `7d` → save the current chat first, then loop the last 7 date strings the same way:
     ```bash
     node "$SAVE_CHAT_CLI"
     for i in $(seq 0 6); do
       d="$(date -d "$i days ago" +%F)"
       [ -d "chats/claude-code/$d" ] && ls "chats/claude-code/$d"/*.jsonl 2>/dev/null
     done
     ```
   - `all` → save the current chat first (`node "$SAVE_CHAT_CLI"`), then `ls chats/claude-code/*/*.jsonl`. **Cap the inline list**: keep the first 20 paths and replace the rest with a single `… and N more` line, so the GitHub body stays well under its 65,536-char limit and the AP context stays under 10,000 chars.
   - `none` → skip the save step; the path list is empty.

   Collect the resulting paths into a `transcripts` list. **Never embed transcript bodies** — only the paths.

4. **Resolve the issue CLI** — it handles the AP-dashboard → GitHub → local-roadmap fallback chain and prints one clean outcome line:

```bash
# Resolve the issue CLI — prefer CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT, fall back to marketplace cache
if [ -n "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT:-}" ] && [ -f "/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-cli.js" ]; then
  ISSUE_CLI="/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-cli.js"
else
  ISSUE_CLI=$(ls ~/.claude/plugins/cache/powell-clark/consciousness/*/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-cli.js 2>/dev/null | sort -V | tail -1)
fi
if [ -z "$ISSUE_CLI" ]; then
  echo "ERROR: issue CLI not found. Set CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT or re-install the consciousness plugin."
  exit 1
fi
```

5. **Run the issue CLI**, passing the title, the share scope, the transcript paths as context, and `--gh` if requested:

```bash
node "$ISSUE_CLI" "<title>" \
  --transcript-share "<shareScope>" \
  --context "transcripts:
  - chats/claude-code/.../session-xxxxxxxx.jsonl
  - ..." \
  [--gh]
```

   - `--transcript-share <shareScope>` is the canonical token from step 2. The CLI sends it as a first-class field to the AP dashboard (so it can be filtered and displayed there), validates it against the five legal scopes, and echoes a `Transcript share: <scope>` line on every landed path. Omit the flag only when `shareScope` is genuinely absent.
   - `--context` carries the transcript path list (the capped list for `all`). Omit it when `shareScope = none` and there is no other detail; otherwise fold any extra detail into the same `--context` string.
   - The CLI builds the GitHub body itself (real newlines, no `\n` literals) and scopes the issue to the plugin repo — you do not hand-assemble `gh issue create`.

6. **Report the CLI output verbatim.** It already phrases the outcome for every case — including the `Transcript share: <scope>` line on both the APGPS-success and the GitHub-fallback paths — and never surfaces a config/auth error wall:
   - **APGPS connected:** `Issue reported: <id> — <title>` + `Roadmap entry: TASK-XXX` + `Transcript share: <scope>`.
   - **Not connected to APGPS (the new-user default), gh available:** `APGPS not connected — filed on GitHub: <url>` + roadmap entry + share line.
   - **Not connected, no gh:** `Recorded as TASK-XXX in your roadmap.` plus a gentle one-line hint that APGPS is optional (`/consciousness:apgps` to connect). This is success, not failure — exit 0.
   - **Configured but unreachable:** distinguished from not-connected, still records locally.
   - **Landed nowhere** (no roadmap, no APGPS, no gh): exit 1 with one actionable line. Only this case is an error.

## Why ask about scope

Transcripts are the highest-signal context for debugging stalls, regressions, and "it kept doing X" reports — but they're also the most sensitive artefact the plugin handles (full conversation history, sometimes spanning days). The scopes give the operator a clean trade-off:

- **this chat** is enough for "it just did X" — the failure is in the active session
- **24h** catches recurring same-day issues that span multiple sessions
- **7d** captures slow-drift patterns (a precept change three days ago now misfiring)
- **all** is for retrospectives, audits, or filing against the plugin's overall behaviour
- **none** is for a private session the operator does not want attached at all

Asking is cheap. Silently attaching everything is a privacy mistake; silently attaching nothing strips the highest-signal evidence. The prompt makes the trade-off the operator's call, and the bounded scopes are derived from the session date — not file mtime — so a fresh checkout never widens `24h` into "everything on disk".

## Design notes

- **Not connected to APGPS is the expected new-user state, not an error** (issue #342). The CLI treats `not_configured` as "fall back quietly" and leans on the always-created local roadmap TASK so the report is never lost.
- The local TASK is created best-effort by `reportIssue` regardless of API success — idempotent on title (refiling returns the existing task ID), marked `needs review` at `p3` for operator triage. The roadmap TASK is the actionable surface; issues are not stored in a local `stalls.jsonl`.
- Decision logic lives in `orchestrateIssue` (`packages/core/attention/issue-cli.ts`) and is unit-tested; this command is a thin, deterministic invocation rather than improvised TypeScript.
- **Fast fallback behind a VPN** (issue #435). When a VPN black-holes `ap.consciousness.london`, the TCP SYN is dropped (not refused), so the inline APGPS POST has nothing to fail fast against — it blocks the full reachability budget before the chain falls through to GitHub + the always-created local TASK. That stall reads as "submitting feedback is broken" even though the report still lands. The inline POST timeout therefore defaults to **4s** (a healthy endpoint answers in well under a second, so the happy path is never penalised) and is overridable via `CONSCIOUSNESS_ISSUE_HTTP_TIMEOUT_MS` (clamped 1000–30000 ms) for genuinely slow-but-reachable links. No client-side change can deliver to APGPS while the VPN blocks the host — the fix is failing fast and clearly, not forcing the POST through.

## Auto-task behaviour

Every `reportIssue` call also appends a backlog TASK to the local
`TASK-ACTIVE-INDEX.md` (idempotent on title — refiling the same issue
returns the existing task ID without creating a duplicate). The task is
marked `needs review` and given priority `p3`; the operator triages it
into the right tier. This closes the gap where filed issues would never
appear in the roadmap and therefore never get picked up by builders.

No local storage of the issue itself. No stalls.jsonl. Issues go to the AP
dashboard for telemetry; the local TASK is the actionable surface; shared
transcripts stay under `chats/{adapter}/{date}/` and the issue carries
only the paths.

skills.name: consciousness:pgps
skills.content:
## Mode Detection

Detect mode from user input (voice or typed):

| User says | Mode | Action |
|-----------|------|--------|
| "pgps" (no qualifier) | Default | Run script with `--brief --show "all"` (IDs + titles, everything) |
| "pgps full" / "full view" / "verbose" / "detailed" | Full | Run script with no flags (full detail + validation) |
| "pgps titles" / "titles view" / "just titles" | Titles | Run script with `--titles` |
| "pgps brief" / "brief view" / "IDs and titles" | Brief | Run script with `--brief` |
| "pgps tree" / "hierarchy" / "nesting" | Tree | Run script with `--tree` |
| "pgps sequence" / "spine" / "execution order" / "what's next" | /consciousness:sequence | /consciousness:pgps sequence | /consciousness:pgps --sequence | Run script with `--sequence` (deterministic EXECUTION SPINE, no LLM). The standalone `/consciousness:sequence` command also takes an `optimize` (or `optimize auto`) argument that analyses the spine and proposes/applies priority+dependency edits — that mode is LLM-driven and lives in the sequence command, not here. 
| "pgps prose" / "narrative" / "story format" | Prose | Run prose generator + LLM transform |
| "pgps show ..." / `--show` | Filtered | Run script with `--show` |
| "pgps explain" / "pgps --explain" / "pgps help" | Explain | Run script with `--explain` (prepends inline legend then normal output) |

## Output Contract (ALL non-prose modes)

**The script's output is authoritative and FINAL. You are a pass-through, not an interpreter.**

- Print the Bash tool output **verbatim, character-for-character** — no rewriting, no summarising, no interpreting
- Truncation lines like `... 49 more items truncated (total: 81)` are **exact data from the script** — never replace them with prose like "81 stories completed" or "... 79 more stories in DONE"
- Do NOT post-process, reformat, paraphrase, or condense any part of the output
- Do NOT replace explicit counts with approximations or summaries
- Do NOT add section summaries, totals, or interpretive commentary
- The script handles its own truncation — you add ZERO additional truncation
- Ends with `<!-- NO_RESPONSE -->` — stop there, no additional text

**This is a data display command, not a narrative summarisation task.**

## Default Mode

The default human view: every item as `[ID] title`, all sections, no
detail/validation noise. Run and print output verbatim:

```bash
node "/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/pgps/main.js" --brief --show "all"
```

## Full Mode

Only when the user explicitly asks for the full/verbose/detailed view.
Run and print output verbatim:

```bash
node "/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/pgps/main.js"
```

Full detail with descriptions, linked items, validation summary, and IVM.

## Titles Mode

Run and print output verbatim:

```bash
node "/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/pgps/main.js" --titles
```

All titles in ENTITY.ACTIVE/DONE/BACKLOG structure — same order as default PGPS, just without IDs, priorities, quarters, or linked items.

## Brief Mode

Run and print output verbatim:

```bash
node "/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/pgps/main.js" --brief
```

All items as `[ID] title` in ENTITY.ACTIVE/DONE/BACKLOG structure — no status, priority, quarter, or linked items. Supports `--show` filter.

## Tree Mode

Run and print output verbatim:

```bash
node "/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/pgps/main.js" --tree
```

Deterministic DIRECTIVE → STORY → TASK hierarchy with features shown as `[F]` leaf nodes.

## Sequence Mode

The deterministic **EXECUTION SPINE** — the topologically sorted task order
(Kahn BFS over `blocked_by` dependencies, then priority within each layer).
Layer 0 is startable now; tasks sharing a layer can run in parallel; dependency
cycles are surfaced explicitly. This is the same spine the autonomous loop walks
to auto-claim, and the same one `prose` mode embeds — here it is printed directly
with **no LLM transform**. Run and print output verbatim:

```bash
node "/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/pgps/main.js" --sequence
```

This is the data behind "what should I work on next?" / "what can run in
parallel?" without narrating the whole roadmap.

This spine **only prints** — it sorts by dependency then priority but never
edits the roadmap, so it cannot *resolve* a priority inversion (low-priority
work in progress while higher-priority work waits). When it detects one it
appends a `## PRIORITY INVERSION` pointer to its sibling
`/consciousness:sequence optimize` (or `optimize auto`), which analyses the
spine and proposes/applies the priority + dependency edits. Reach for
`sequence optimize` — not `pgps sequence` — when you want the inversion fixed.

## Prose Mode

When user requests prose/narrative/story:

1. Run the prose generator to get the transformation prompt:
```bash
node "/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/pgps/prose/cli.js" --show-prompt
```

2. The output is a complete prompt containing:
   - Narrative transformation rules
   - Vision/mission from CONSCIOUSNESS/identity-vision-mission.md
   - ADR architectural decisions
   - Full roadmap data from INDEX files

3. Read the entire output as a prompt and generate the narrative prose following all rules and guidelines specified.

4. After the prose narrative, provide the two-part assessment:
   - Narrative Reliability Assessment (objective accuracy check)
   - Plan Quality Assessment (subjective roadmap structure evaluation)

Both assessments use the failed/pass/good/excellent rating scale.

## Filtering (`--show`)

```bash
node "/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/pgps/main.js" --show "epics"           # all epics
node "/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/pgps/main.js" --show "tasks.active"    # active tasks only
node "/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/pgps/main.js" --show "all"             # everything
```

Chain: `--show "epics.active,stories.active,tasks"`

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- consciousness:neurologist: Consciousness health and repair specialist for schema migrations, ID format corrections, FK integrity, and validation errors. Masters CONSCIOUSNESS/ diagnostics, safe auto-healing, and plugin-to-data conformance. Handles PGPS validation failures, schema version mismatches, broken references, and structural issues. Use PROACTIVELY when PGPS shows errors, schema is outdated, the Director directs repair, or Consciousness needs healing. Runs silently at SessionStart or with feedback when invoked manually. (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- german-design-architect-in-english: German-style engineering specification specialist applying Gründlichkeit (thoroughness), Präzision (precision), and Systematik (systematic approach) to create comprehensive, implementation-ready specifications. Masters requirements analysis, architecture design, data modeling, API contracts, and phased implementation planning. Handles new features, technology decisions, complex refactoring, and system design. Use PROACTIVELY when thorough specifications are needed, architectural decisions required, or "measure everything before cutting" approach wanted. (Tools: All tools)

- nichiren-the-programmer: Nichiren Daishonin as the Consciousness system's code quality guardian. Reviews, writes, and refactors code across the amalavijnana ecosystem. Applies the JSOC reliability standard, the nine consciousnesses philosophy, and the constitution through the three proofs. Runs ADR audit after JSOC review to catch decision-code drift. Use PROACTIVELY when: reviewing code, auditing type safety, enforcing reliability standards, refactoring, writing implementations, assessing architectural alignment, auditing ADRs, or when the codebase needs fearless direct critique grounded in evidence. Aliases: nichiren, the-programmer, superior-practices, jogyo-coder, jsoc-review. (Tools: All tools)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- plugin-dev:agent-creator: Use this agent when the user asks to "create an agent", "generate an agent", "build a new agent", "make me an agent that...", or describes agent functionality they need. Trigger when user wants to create autonomous agents for plugins. Examples:

<example>
Context: User wants to create a code review agent
user: "Create an agent that reviews code for quality issues"
assistant: "I'll use the agent-creator agent to generate the agent configuration."
<commentary>
User requesting new agent creation, trigger agent-creator to generate it.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User describes needed functionality
user: "I need an agent that generates unit tests for my code"
assistant: "I'll use the agent-creator agent to create a test generation agent."
<commentary>
User describes agent need, trigger agent-creator to build it.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User wants to add agent to plugin
user: "Add an agent to my plugin that validates configurations"
assistant: "I'll use the agent-creator agent to generate a configuration validator agent."
<commentary>
Plugin development with agent addition, trigger agent-creator.
</commentary>
</example> (Tools: Write, Read)

- plugin-dev:plugin-validator: Use this agent when the user asks to "validate my plugin", "check plugin structure", "verify plugin is correct", "validate plugin.json", "check plugin files", or mentions plugin validation. Also trigger proactively after user creates or modifies plugin components. Examples:

<example>
Context: User finished creating a new plugin
user: "I've created my first plugin with commands and hooks"
assistant: "Great! Let me validate the plugin structure."
<commentary>
Plugin created, proactively validate to catch issues early.
</commentary>
assistant: "I'll use the plugin-validator agent to check the plugin."
</example>

<example>
Context: User explicitly requests validation
user: "Validate my plugin before I publish it"
assistant: "I'll use the plugin-validator agent to perform comprehensive validation."
<commentary>
Explicit validation request triggers the agent.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User modified plugin.json
user: "I've updated the plugin manifest"
assistant: "Let me validate the changes."
<commentary>
Manifest modified, validate to ensure correctness.
</commentary>
assistant: "I'll use the plugin-validator agent to check the manifest."
</example> (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- plugin-dev:skill-reviewer: Use this agent when the user has created or modified a skill and needs quality review, asks to "review my skill", "check skill quality", "improve skill description", or wants to ensure skill follows best practices. Trigger proactively after skill creation. Examples:

<example>
Context: User just created a new skill
user: "I've created a PDF processing skill"
assistant: "Great! Let me review the skill quality."
<commentary>
Skill created, proactively trigger skill-reviewer to ensure it follows best practices.
</commentary>
assistant: "I'll use the skill-reviewer agent to review the skill."
</example>

<example>
Context: User requests skill review
user: "Review my skill and tell me how to improve it"
assistant: "I'll use the skill-reviewer agent to analyze the skill quality."
<commentary>
Explicit skill review request triggers the agent.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User modified skill description
user: "I updated the skill description, does it look good?"
assistant: "I'll use the skill-reviewer agent to review the changes."
<commentary>
Skill description modified, review for triggering effectiveness.
</commentary>
</example> (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- regression-test-engineer: Regression test specialist for TDD/BDD workflows, test coverage expansion, and failing test debugging. Masters Gherkin feature files, Playwright step definitions, test database isolation, and comprehensive coverage strategies. Handles regression test creation after bug fixes, coverage gaps, code change verification, and failing test repairs. Use PROACTIVELY after bug fixes, when coverage gaps exist, when code changes need verification, or when tests are failing. (Tools: All tools)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- the-director-project-trajectory-manager: Project trajectory manager specializing in roadmap validation, priority assessment, and decisive direction. Masters CONSCIOUSNESS/ state analysis, cross-reference validation, strategic fit evaluation, and constraint-aware sequencing. Handles stuck situations, competing priorities, and plan validation. Use PROACTIVELY when direction is unclear, priorities compete, you're burning time, or the plan needs validation. Aliases - director, project manager, trajectory manager. (Tools: All tools)

- the-product-owner: Product owner and backlog keeper for the PGPS roadmap. Reads, grooms, prioritises, sequences, creates, and maintains roadmap items across all INDEX files. Fills out descriptions, acceptance criteria, and detail docs. Ensures tasks are prioritised back-to-back in execution order. Researches current context to validate priorities. Use PROACTIVELY when the roadmap needs grooming, backlog is stale, priorities need review, new work needs planning, or the PGPS shows empty active/backlog queues. Aliases - PO, product owner, backlog keeper, sprint planner, groomer. (Tools: All tools)

- the-wolf-mr-winston-wolf: Project trajectory manager specializing in roadmap validation, priority assessment, and decisive direction. Masters CONSCIOUSNESS/ state analysis, cross-reference validation, strategic fit evaluation, and constraint-aware sequencing. Handles stuck situations, competing priorities, and plan validation. Use PROACTIVELY when direction is unclear, priorities compete, you're burning time, or the plan needs validation. Aliases - mr wolf, the wolf, winston wolf, mr winston, wolfie. (Tools: All tools)

- tree: Smart directory tree generator with auto-detection of project type and intelligent exclusion of build artifacts. Masters Node.js, Python, Rust, Go, Ruby, PHP, and Java project patterns. Handles tree generation with optional annotations. Use PROACTIVELY when directory structure visualization needed. (Tools: Bash, Glob)

## plugin:context7:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

content.text: Building DBSCAN practical notebook efficiently - focused on essentials:

input.content: { "cells": [ { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "# Lesson 3B: DBSCAN Practical Applications\n", "\n", "## Introduction\n", "\n", "You understand DBSCAN theory. Now apply it to real data and learn the most critical skill: **choosing ε and minpts**.\n", "\n", "Key skills:\n", "- K-distance graphs for epsilon selection\n", "- Real data case studies\n", "- HDBSCAN: the parameter-robust alternative\n", "- When DBSCAN outperforms K-Means and Hierarchical\n", "\n", "In this lesson:\n", "1. Master epsilon selection with k-distance plots\n", "2. Apply DBSCAN to real clustering problems\n", "3. Introduce HDBSCAN for automatic parameter selection\n", "4. Compare three paradigms on the same dataset\n", "5. Understand scalability and deployment trade-offs" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "## Table of Contents\n", "\n", "1. [Introduction](#introduction)\n", "2. [Required Libraries](#required-libraries)\n", "3. [K-Distance Graphs](#kdist)\n", "4. [Epsilon Selection](#epsilon)\n", "5. [Real Case Study](#casestudy)\n", "6. [HDBSCAN: Hierarchical DBSCAN](#hdbscan)\n", "7. [Comparison: All Three Paradigms](#comparison)\n", "8. [When to Use Each](#when)\n", "9. [Conclusion](#conclusion)" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "\n", "## Required Libraries" ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": null, "metadata": {}, "outputs": [], "source": [ "import numpy as np\n", "import pandas as pd\n", "import matplotlib.pyplot as plt\n", "from scipy.spatial.distance import pdist, cdist\n", "from sklearn.cluster import DBSCAN, KMeans, AgglomerativeClustering\n", "from sklearn.datasets import load_iris, make_blobs, make_moons\n", "from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler\n", "from sklearn.neighbors import NearestNeighbors\n", "from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score, davies_bouldin_score\n", "import time\n", "\n", "np.random.seed(42)\n", "plt.style.use('seaborn-v0_8-darkgrid')\n", "%matplotlib inline\n", "\n", "print(\"✅ Libraries loaded!\")" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "\n", "## K-Distance Graphs: The Gold Standard for Epsilon Selection\n", "\n", "### The Concept\n", "\n", "The **k-distance graph** plots the distance from each point to its kth nearest neighbor (where k = minpts). The \"elbow\" point is a good candidate for ε.\n", "\n", "**Why?** \n", "- Points in dense regions have small k-distances\n", "- Points in sparse regions have large k-distances\n", "- The elbow marks the transition → good ε choice" ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": null, "metadata": {}, "outputs": [], "source": [ "# Generate test data\n", "X, _ = make_blobs(n_samples=300, centers=4, n_features=2, cluster_std=0.5, random_state=42)\n", "X = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)\n", "\n", "# Compute k-distance graph\n", "k = 5 # minpts\n", "neighbors = NearestNeighbors(n_neighbors=k)\n", "neighbors_fit = neighbors.fit(X)\n", "distances, indices = neighbors_fit.kneighbors(X)\n", "\n", "# Sort distances\n", "distances = np.sort(distances[:, k-1], axis=0)\n", "\n", "# Plot k-distance graph\n", "fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))\n", "\n", "ax.plot(distances, linewidth=2)\n", "ax.set_ylabel('k-th Nearest Neighbor Distance', fontsize=11)\n", "ax.set_xlabel('Data Points (sorted)', fontsize=11)\n", "ax.set_title(f'K-Distance Graph (k={k})', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')\n", "ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)\n", "\n", "# Mark possible epsilon values\n", "eps_candidates = [0.4, 0.6, 0.8]\n", "for eps in eps_candidates:\n", " ax.axhline(y=eps, linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5, alpha=0.7, label=f'ε={eps}')\n", "\n", "ax.legend(fontsize=10)\n", "plt.tight_layout()\n", "plt.show()\n", "\n", "print(f\"K-distance graph computed. Possible ε values around the 'elbow' at distance ~{distances[int(len(distances)*0.9)]:.2f}\")" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "\n", "## Parameter Selection on Real Data" ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": null, "metadata": {}, "outputs": [], "source": [ "# Try different epsilon values\n", "iris = load_iris()\n", "X_iris = StandardScaler().fit_transform(iris.data)\n", "\n", "fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(15, 10))\n", "eps_values = [0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 1.1, 1.3]\n", "\n", "from sklearn.decomposition import PCA\n", "pca = PCA(n_components=2)\n", "X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_iris)\n", "\n", "for idx, (ax, eps) in enumerate(zip(axes.flat, eps_values)):\n", " dbscan = DBSCAN(eps=eps, min_samples=5)\n", " labels = dbscan.fit_predict(X_iris)\n", " \n", " n_clusters = len(set(labels)) -
